title: The Good Samaritan

jokes.text: The concept behind phones is that we all have absolutely nothing to say and we've got to talk to someone about it right now.
jokes.setup: Listing all the modern phone technology innovations
jokes.punchline: The ironic revelation that despite all this technology, we have nothing meaningful to communicate
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational humor with perfect setup/payoff structure. The lengthy technology list builds to a brilliant insight about human nature.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We've got call waiting, we've got caller ID, we've got three-way calling, we've got speed dial. We've got all this technology, all these innovations in communication. And you know what? We have absolutely nothing to say to each other.

jokes.text: You gotta give people a chance to miss you a little!
jokes.setup: Describing the constant communication cycle of modern life
jokes.punchline: The simple wisdom that constant contact prevents missing someone
jokes.explanation: Great insight delivered with Jerry's characteristic wisdom. Highly quotable and universally relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what nobody does anymore? They don't miss anybody. You can't miss someone because you're always in contact with them. They text you, you text them back, they call you, you email them... It's constant contact. You never get that thing where you really miss somebody.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Missing someone was actually a good feeling. It meant you cared about them. Now you can't miss anybody because you know exactly what they're doing at all times. They're sitting on the couch eating cereal.

jokes.text: It's safer. Plus, it's more annoying to the other person.
jokes.setup: Jerry complaining about speakerphone use
jokes.punchline: George's honest admission that annoying others is a bonus feature
jokes.explanation: Perfect George character moment - his petty, antisocial tendencies revealed as casual benefits.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I use speakerphone specifically to annoy people. It's like a bonus feature I discovered.

jokes.text: How could you not give a thank-you wave? Hey, buddy, where's my thank-you wave?
jokes.setup: Driver lets someone merge but gets no acknowledgment
jokes.punchline: George's indignant demand for the social courtesy
jokes.explanation: Classic road rage/social etiquette observation with George's characteristic outrage over minor slights.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You see, this is what I'm talking about. A simple thank you. A wave of the hand. A nod of the head. You merge in front of me, the least you can do is acknowledge it.

jokes.text: What are you, yellow?
jokes.setup: Jerry hesitant to follow hit-and-run driver
jokes.punchline: George using playground insult to goad Jerry into action
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing George's ability to manipulate through childish taunts.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you, chicken? Are you a chicken? Bawk bawk bawk bawk!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not a chicken, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Then go after him!

jokes.text: Your left rear tire's a little low. That can affect the performance of the twin I-beam suspension, not to mention your rack-and-pinion steering.
jokes.setup: Jerry approaches the hit-and-run driver to confront him
jokes.punchline: Instead chickens out and gives car maintenance advice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection with Jerry's cowardly pivot to helpful car advice. The technical car terms add absurdist humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hey! You hit my car and just drove away!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, your tire pressure looks a little low. You should really get that checked out. And your oil—when's the last time you changed it?

jokes.text: So I wound up going out for a decaf cappuccino with her.
jokes.setup: Audience expects Jerry to tell about confronting the driver
jokes.punchline: Reveals he ended up having coffee with her instead
jokes.explanation: Perfect plot twist delivered with Jerry's casual tone, completely subverting expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking I'm gonna go out there, really let her have it. Tell her off, you know? I'm all pumped up, I'm ready to go.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I walk up to the car, I tap on the window... She rolls it down, and you know what? She's got the most beautiful smile.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Next thing I know, we're at a coffee shop. We're having cappuccino, talking about her day.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I completely forgot why I was mad in the first place.

jokes.text: I'm speechless, speechless. I have no speech.
jokes.setup: George reacting to Jerry's story
jokes.punchline: Escalating ways to express being speechless, ending with redundant 'no speech'
jokes.explanation: Classic George over-the-top reaction with escalating redundancy that becomes funny through excess.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm speechless. I have no speech.

jokes.text: Love when they touch your arm. I can't get enough of that.
jokes.setup: Jerry describes woman touching his arm when she laughs
jokes.punchline: George's overly enthusiastic agreement about this simple gesture
jokes.explanation: Relatable male observation delivered with George's characteristic desperation and over-enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, that's huge! That's huge! If a woman touches your arm, you're in! You're in like Flynn!

jokes.text: Let's not even analyze it.
jokes.setup: George asking why they love arm touching
jokes.punchline: Jerry refusing to analyze, which is ironic given the show's nature
jokes.explanation: Subtle meta-humor about not over-analyzing, which is exactly what the show does constantly.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why do we love arm touching so much?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not analyzing it. That's where it ends.

jokes.text: Oh, you dig?
jokes.setup: Kramer says 'I dig' about keeping the affair secret
jokes.punchline: Jerry's mock surprise at Kramer's dated slang choice
jokes.explanation: Mild language humor poking fun at outdated expressions, delivered with Jerry's signature rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What, 'I dig'? What is this, 1973? Are you going to tell me you're going to keep this affair secret and then ask me if I want to go to a fondue party?

jokes.text: That's why I've never been in a long-term relationship. I never had a good meeting story.
jokes.setup: Discussion about couples needing good meeting stories
jokes.punchline: George attributing his relationship failures to lack of interesting meeting stories
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - blaming romantic failure on narrative inadequacy rather than personal issues.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I think that's my problem. I don't have a good meeting story. That's why I can't get a relationship to work. Women need to hear a good meeting story.

jokes.text: What am I drinking, milk?
jokes.setup: Jerry weighing whether to pursue someone with questionable morals
jokes.punchline: Sudden awareness that he's drinking milk, suggesting innocence/naivety
jokes.explanation: Brilliant non-sequitur that connects drink choice to moral stance. Unexpected and perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line:
I shouldn't be dating her. She's a bad person.

But I'm just drinking milk...

jokes.text: Queens?
jokes.setup: Jerry claiming he followed someone to Queens
jokes.punchline: Elaine's shocked reaction to Jerry going to Queens
jokes.explanation: NYC geography humor - treating Queens like a foreign country. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You went to Queens?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Nobody goes to Queens.

jokes.text: So I said to him, 'Whatever's necessary.'
jokes.setup: Jerry fabricating confrontation story
jokes.punchline: His fake tough-guy response sounds like bad movie dialogue
jokes.explanation: Jerry's attempt at sounding tough comes off as theatrical and unconvincing, fitting his character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I said to him, 'You want a piece of me? Because you can't handle what's coming. I've got moves you've never seen before.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I mean, who talks like that? That's terrible dialogue.

jokes.text: I am without speech.
jokes.setup: Elaine reacting to Jerry's heroic story
jokes.punchline: Echoing George's earlier 'speechless' escalation pattern
jokes.explanation: Direct callback to George's earlier speech pattern, but less funny the second time.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait, so you actually did that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, I couldn't just stand there.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's... that's incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It was nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Nothing? Jerry, you're speechless!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not speechless.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You can't even speak!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm speaking right now!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: See? You're so speechless you don't even know you're speechless!

jokes.text: Tell her about the shoving.
jokes.setup: George wants Jerry to embellish the fake story further
jokes.punchline: Adding fictional details to make Jerry sound more heroic
jokes.explanation: George as enabler of Jerry's lie, showing his investment in the fictional heroic narrative.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You gotta make yourself sound more heroic. Come on, you saved her life!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you want me to say?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Tell her about the sea. How you wrestled it. How you grabbed its hair and you kept it down under the water until it stopped moving.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The sea doesn't have hair, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Don't tell her that! Make it sound good!

jokes.text: so I went into a karate stance.
jokes.setup: Jerry describing being pushed by the driver
jokes.punchline: Claims he used karate, which is completely uncharacteristic
jokes.explanation: Jerry pretending to be action hero is inherently funny given his mild-mannered character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So he pushes me, and I'm like, 'Hey!' And I do a karate chop.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know karate. I've never taken karate in my life.

jokes.text: That's where England used to send their convicts. But not anymore.
jokes.setup: Kramer suggesting hit-and-run drivers be sent to Australia
jokes.punchline: Historical correction about England's penal colonies, delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer - bizarre solution followed by oddly specific historical knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why don't they just send them to Australia?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Australia? Kramer, they sent criminals to Australia.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So? That's where they sent them. It was a penal colony.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. England sent their criminals to Australia. We send ours to Australia, we're not gonna dump them all in the same place. That doesn't make any sense.

jokes.text: I was watching Entertainment Tonight and suddenly I got dizzy and hit my head on the coffee table.
jokes.setup: Kramer has mysterious head injury
jokes.punchline: Bizarre explanation involving Entertainment Tonight
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Mary Hart seizure subplot with appropriately weird Kramer medical mystery.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I was watching Entertainment Tonight, and I leaned in real close to the TV to see if that was really Katie Couric, and I hit my head on the corner of the table.

jokes.text: They're always trying to make me feel like their life is so much better.
jokes.setup: Elaine explaining why she needs George at dinner
jokes.punchline: Insight about married couples' subtle superiority displays
jokes.explanation: Sharp social observation about married/single dynamics delivered with Elaine's characteristic insight.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I need George at dinner because married couples have this thing where they subtly lord it over you. You know, little comments, little looks. They're like, 'Oh, you're still single?' It's insufferable. But if George is there, at least I have someone to commiserate with. We can exchange knowing glances across the table.

jokes.text: You know, I have a very exciting life. It's very exciting.
jokes.setup: Elaine defending her single life
jokes.punchline: Over-protesting about excitement reveals insecurity
jokes.explanation: The repetition and overemphasis reveals Elaine's defensiveness about her single status.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm not lonely! I have a great life. I go out, I have fun, I meet people. There's plenty of excitement!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Why does everyone keep asking me if I'm seeing anyone? I'm fine being single. Totally fine. More than fine. I'm great!

jokes.text: Eduardo Caroccio.
jokes.setup: Elaine pressed for her fictional matador boyfriend's name
jokes.punchline: Makes up an over-the-top Spanish-sounding name on the spot
jokes.explanation: Elaine's improvised fake name sounds appropriately fabricated and stereotypical.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: His name is... Poppie.

jokes.text: They don't really fight the bull. They avoid fighting the bull.
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to explain bullfighting
jokes.punchline: Accurate but deflating description of the 'sport'
jokes.explanation: Clever deconstruction of bullfighting mystique while Elaine digs deeper into her lie.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, it's not really a sport. It's just a man in a fancy outfit waving a cape at a confused animal until it gets tired and they kill it. That's it. That's bullfighting.

jokes.text: Bread.
jokes.setup: Uncomfortable moment during Elaine's bullfighter story
jokes.punchline: George's awkward topic change to food
jokes.explanation: Perfect awkward silence filler - George's social discomfort manifesting as random food request.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So uh, you ever notice how the bread at that Italian place is really good? Like, really good bread.

jokes.text: I wasn't gonna say anything, but I saw he wasn't gonna open his mouth.
jokes.setup: George explaining why he said 'God bless you'
jokes.punchline: His righteousness about filling social courtesy gaps
jokes.explanation: George positioning himself as socially responsible while being completely inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about this 'God bless you' thing. Somebody sneezes, you say 'God bless you.' It's a social courtesy, a gap-filler. And somebody has to do it. Somebody has to maintain these standards or society breaks down.

jokes.text: But from what I understand, not a very good driver. Hits everything on the road, but always leaves a note.
jokes.setup: Talking about Anthony Quinn as an actor
jokes.punchline: Absurd claim about his driving habits connecting to hit-and-run theme
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist tangent connecting celebrity gossip to the episode's central theme in unexpected way.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Anthony Quinn? The guy's a maniac behind the wheel. I'm telling you, half his movies are about hit-and-runs because that's what he does in real life. The man treats every red light like a suggestion.

jokes.text: Quinn said he never felt so good as when he left a note after smacking into a car.
jokes.setup: Continuing the Anthony Quinn driving story
jokes.punchline: Fake quote about moral satisfaction from responsibility
jokes.explanation: Extension of the absurd Quinn bit with moral messaging that contrasts with Robin's behavior.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I was thinking about what you said. About responsibility and moral satisfaction. And you know what? Anthony Quinn was right. 'There is no greater satisfaction than the knowledge that you have done your duty.' That's a real quote. I looked it up.

jokes.text: I don't think I'm special. My mother always said I'm not special.
jokes.setup: Michael challenging George about saying 'God bless you'
jokes.punchline: George's sad deflection referencing his mother's criticism
jokes.explanation: Perfect George family dysfunction reference that simultaneously deflects and reveals his psychology.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My mother never said 'God bless you' to me in her life.

jokes.text: Kirk Douglas. Now, there's another very bad driver. But he's such an unbelievable guy when he hits someone, he doesn't leave a note. He sits in his car and waits for the other person.
jokes.setup: Continuing the celebrity driver theme
jokes.punchline: Escalating Kirk Douglas to even more responsible behavior
jokes.explanation: Escalates the absurd celebrity driving bit to even more extreme responsibility, creating funny contrast.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking, if Kirk Douglas can parallel park, maybe he can be trusted with something bigger.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know, a school bus? A nuclear power plant?

jokes.text: Because as the husband, he has the right of first refusal.
jokes.setup: Analyzing the 'God bless you' social dynamics
jokes.punchline: Applying business contract terms to social courtesies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant application of legal/business terminology to mundane social interaction. Pure Seinfeld analysis.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I realized? 'God bless you' is basically a verbal contract. You sneeze, I say 'God bless you,' and now we're bound by social law.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: If I don't say it, I've breached the agreement. You can actually sue me for emotional damages.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And if you sneeze again and I don't say it the second time? That's a repeated violation. Now we're in arbitration.

jokes.text: unless she's one of these multiple sneezers and he's holding his 'God bless you' in abeyance until she completes the series.
jokes.setup: Jerry's 'God bless you' analysis continues
jokes.punchline: Complex etiquette rules involving multiple sneezes and timing strategies
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalation of social analysis with technical language like 'abeyance' and 'series' applied to sneezing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, the 'God bless you' thing, it's very tricky. Because if someone sneezes, you say 'God bless you.' But what if they sneeze again? Do you say it again? How many times do you say 'God bless you'? Is there a limit?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I think after the third sneeze, you're off the hook. You've done your part. You've blessed them enough. At that point, they're on their own with God.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And the timing is crucial. You have to say it right after the sneeze. If you wait too long, it doesn't count. It's like a blessing statute of limitations.

jokes.text: What if she's having an off night?
jokes.setup: Jerry analyzing sneeze patterns
jokes.punchline: George treating sneezing like athletic performance with good and bad nights
jokes.explanation: Perfect extension of the sneeze analysis treating it like sports performance evaluation.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I've been noticing a pattern with sneezing. Some days I sneeze a lot, some days I don't sneeze at all.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, well that's like anything else. You have good nights and bad nights. Last night I had a good sneeze night. Three, four times. Really came out strong.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're treating sneezing like it's a performance.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It is a performance! You gotta commit to it. Can't be tentative with a sneeze.

jokes.text: It's a stupid 'stuperstition.'
jokes.setup: Michael criticizing 'God bless you' traditions
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing 'superstition' as 'stuperstition'
jokes.explanation: Simple portmanteau word joke that's mildly clever but not particularly sophisticated.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What is this 'God bless you' thing anyway? It's a stuperstition!

jokes.text: You should say, 'You're so good-looking.'
jokes.setup: Michael saying what's better than 'God bless you'
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate compliment as sneeze response
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd alternative to 'God bless you' that becomes running element in episode.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what's better than 'God bless you'? Instead of saying 'God bless you,' I'm gonna say, 'You're very attractive.' Think about it—a guy sneezes, you say, 'You're very attractive.' He'd be like, 'What? Why would you say that?' 'Well, you sneezed. That's what I say now.'

jokes.text: Mary Hart's voice! There's something about Mary Hart's voice that's giving you seizures. Just like that woman in Albany!
jokes.setup: Kramer has mysterious head injuries while watching TV
jokes.punchline: Jerry realizes Entertainment Tonight host's voice causes medical condition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist medical mystery resolution with real-world reference to actual news story about Mary Hart causing seizures.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. You got these head injuries watching TV?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was watching Entertainment Tonight and—

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Entertainment Tonight? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't know, man. Every time that host comes on, my head starts pounding. It's like this involuntary reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're telling me you have a physical condition triggered by a TV host's voice?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's exactly what I'm telling you!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, maybe you should see a doctor about that.

jokes.text: It's so adult. It's like with stockings and martinis and William Holden.
jokes.setup: George considering having an affair
jokes.punchline: His romanticized, old Hollywood vision of adultery
jokes.explanation: Perfect George fantasy thinking - romanticizing morally questionable behavior through movie imagery.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of having an affair.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, like in those old movies. I'd have a secret apartment, maybe in the city. There'd be a woman waiting for me, wearing a negligee, jazz playing in the background.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'd loosen my tie, she'd hand me a martini... it would be very sophisticated, very Continental.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Of course, I'd never actually do it. The logistics alone would be impossible. I can barely coordinate my schedule as it is.

jokes.text: On the other hand, it probably wouldn't cost me any money.
jokes.setup: George's romantic fantasy about affairs
jokes.punchline: Immediately shifts to practical financial considerations
jokes.explanation: Perfect George character moment - romantic thoughts immediately undermined by cheapness concerns.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm fantasizing about having an affair. You know, the passionate encounters, the secret rendezvous, sneaking around...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But then I think about it practically. Where am I gonna find the time? And the money? Do you know how expensive it is to maintain a second relationship? The dinners, the hotel rooms, the lies...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can barely afford this one.

jokes.text: It's like hitting a car and driving away without leaving a note.
jokes.setup: George saying affairs are despicable
jokes.punchline: Uses the exact scenario that started everything as moral comparison
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that brings the hit-and-run theme full circle as moral metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Affairs are despicable. I mean, how could anyone do that? It's wrong, it's immoral, it's... it's betrayal of the highest order.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, it's exactly like when I was with Susan and I... well, never mind.

jokes.text: The blond with the blue sweatpants.
jokes.setup: Kramer mentions girl across the street by description
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate recognition of this very specific description
jokes.explanation: Sets up the running gag of identifying the woman by her clothing rather than more normal descriptors.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: There's this girl across the street, she's got red hair, she's very pretty, she's got like a really nice figure...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, I know exactly who you're talking about.

jokes.text: Wait, wait, wait. How do you know it's not John Tesh?
jokes.setup: Discussion about Mary Hart causing seizures
jokes.punchline: Suggesting her Entertainment Tonight co-host might be the culprit instead
jokes.explanation: Mild extension of the Mary Hart bit but less inspired than the original revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What if it's not Mary Hart? What if it's John Tesh? What if John Tesh's hair is so shiny it's causing seizures?

jokes.text: It's too bad you can't say anything because of Angela.
jokes.setup: Jerry discovering his crush owns the damaged car
jokes.punchline: Kramer pointing out the ironic moral bind Jerry is in
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Jerry's moral dilemma - having to choose between protecting Robin and pursuing Becky.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So you're not gonna tell her about the car?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I can't tell her! She'll never go out with me.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So you're just gonna lie to her?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not lying, I'm just... not mentioning it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's the same thing, Jerry. That's the same thing.

jokes.text: Angela. Lousy thug.
jokes.setup: George realizing moral implications of the affair
jokes.punchline: Calling Robin (Angela) names to justify his decision
jokes.explanation: Classic George moral flip-flopping - now demonizing Robin to feel better about betraying her.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She's not a real person. She's like the Bizarro Jerry. Everything I like, she's the opposite.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She's a bald woman. A bald woman! I'm doing the world a favor.

jokes.text: I actually owe it to society to do something about this!
jokes.setup: George building his case against Robin
jokes.punchline: Escalating to civic duty justification
jokes.explanation: Perfect George rationalization escalation - from personal to societal moral obligation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She stole my marble rye! My marble rye! Do you know what that means? That's not just bread, that's a statement. That says, 'I'm willing to break the law for carbohydrates.'

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm calling the police. It's my civic duty. If we don't stand up to bread thieves now, where does it end? Tomorrow it's marble rye, next week it's the whole bakery!

jokes.text: saying 'God bless you' to every Tom, Dick and Harry, at great personal risk.
jokes.setup: George building himself up morally
jokes.punchline: Treating 'God bless you' as dangerous heroic act
jokes.explanation: George inflating his minor social courtesy into an act of courage and self-sacrifice.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm out there, Jerry, and I'm loving every minute of it. I'm really becoming a man. I'm taking responsibility for my life. I'm doing all these things that I never thought I could do.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's great, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what I did yesterday? I said 'God bless you' to a man who sneezed on the street. A complete stranger. He didn't ask for it. I just did it. And I felt like a hero.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. I must be crazy. What have I done?
jokes.setup: George after talking to Becky about the car damage
jokes.punchline: Immediate regret and panic after his moral stance
jokes.explanation: Typical George flip-flop from righteous confidence to immediate regret once he acts.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what? You're right. I'm going to tell her. I'm going to go right up to her and I'm going to tell her that I hit her car.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going to be honest. I'm going to be straightforward. No lies, no evasions. Just the truth.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. What am I doing? What is wrong with me? She's going to kill me. She's going to absolutely kill me!

jokes.text: You didn't commit adultery. I did.
jokes.setup: George saying he committed adultery
jokes.punchline: Robin's matter-of-fact correction about who actually did what
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan correction of George's dramatic self-blame with technical accuracy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I committed adultery.

dialogue.speaker: Robin
dialogue.line: No, you didn't. You had sex with another woman.

jokes.text: In three years with Michael, not one 'God bless you.'
jokes.setup: Robin talking about her marriage problems
jokes.punchline: Using the 'God bless you' issue as relationship indicator
jokes.explanation: Decent callback linking the 'God bless you' theme to relationship dynamics.

jokes.text: Is she with your bald friend from the other night?
jokes.setup: Michael suspicious about his wife's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: Describing George only by his most prominent physical feature
jokes.explanation: Straightforward physical description humor, less sophisticated than other character observations.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Where have you been?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know you've been seeing someone else.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? No, I haven't!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Don't lie to me. I saw you with that bald guy.

jokes.text: I'm gonna sew his ass to his face!
jokes.setup: Michael threatening George
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely specific and impossible threat
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully creative and absurd threat that's both violent and impossible. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I'm gonna twist his neck so hard his lips will be his eyebrows!
jokes.setup: Michael continuing his threats
jokes.punchline: Even more anatomically impossible threat
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the absurd threat pattern with vivid impossible imagery.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Even more anatomically impossible threat

jokes.text: You're so good-looking.
jokes.setup: Michael making violent threats
jokes.punchline: George using Michael's own 'better than God bless you' line
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and character moment - George deflecting violence with Michael's own absurd suggestion.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny you should say that, because I've been thinking - when someone sneezes, 'God bless you' is just so pedestrian. I've come up with something much better.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going to say 'You're very attractive.'

jokes.text: I'll pluck your body hairs out with my teeth!
jokes.setup: Michael continuing threats despite George's compliment
jokes.punchline: Disgusting and impractical threat involving dental hair removal
jokes.explanation: Continues the absurd threat pattern but getting a bit repetitive by this point.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's a great threat.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, I'm gonna pull out all your teeth and play hockey with your gums.

jokes.text: French Connection kind of thing. You know, sort of a Popeye Doyle chase through the city.
jokes.setup: Elaine describing Jerry's 'heroic' confrontation
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to famous movie chase scene
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference that inflates Jerry's mundane story to action movie proportions.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So Jerry finally confronts this guy, right? And he's like, 'You can't just cut in line like that!' I'm thinking, 'Oh my God, this is it. This is my moment. Jerry's actually going to do something.'

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But then the guy just walks away. Jerry stands there for like two seconds, and suddenly he's telling me it was just like that scene in 'The French Connection.' You know, the famous car chase?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm like, 'Jerry, the only thing chasing you was your own delusion.'

jokes.text: He said he was gonna sew your ass to your face.
jokes.setup: Jerry reporting Michael's threat to George
jokes.punchline: Repeating the memorably absurd threat
jokes.explanation: Callback to the great threat but less impactful the second time around.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He said he's going to kill you.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Kill me? What did I do?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He said, 'I'm going to take that keith and break it in half.'

jokes.text: You lie! How hard is it to lie!
jokes.setup: Jerry failed to cover for George properly
jokes.punchline: George outraged that Jerry can't lie effectively
jokes.explanation: Great George moment - treating dishonesty as a basic life skill Jerry should have mastered.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You couldn't lie? You couldn't just make up some simple lie?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not a good liar, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm George. I'm unemployed and I live with my parents.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: If I can lie, you can lie!

jokes.text: until you got her juiced up with your story about the affair with the matador!
jokes.setup: George blaming Jerry for the situation
jokes.punchline: Blaming Elaine's fake matador story for inspiring the affair
jokes.explanation: Decent callback connecting Elaine's lie to the affair, showing how all storylines interconnect.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is all your fault!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My fault? How is this my fault?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You told Elaine that story about the matador, she told it to my wife, my wife thought it was romantic, and now look at me!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I was just making conversation!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, your conversation cost me my marriage!

jokes.text: Eduardo Caroccio. That's good. That's very good. Kind of just rolls off the tongue.
jokes.setup: Elaine caught in her matador lie
jokes.punchline: Sarcastically complimenting her own improvised name
jokes.explanation: Elaine acknowledging the absurdity of her made-up name with self-aware sarcasm.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, what a name! Poppie is gonna love that!

jokes.text: Perhaps, Zabar's. Or Ray's Pizza.
jokes.setup: Where would a single girl meet a matador on the Upper West Side?
jokes.punchline: Suggesting mundane NYC locations for exotic romance
jokes.explanation: Great NYC-specific humor contrasting exotic romance with pedestrian local establishments.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Where would a single girl meet a matador on the Upper West Side?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: The grocery store? The dry cleaners? Maybe at a bus stop?

jokes.text: $875? That's right.
jokes.setup: Expected expensive car damage from hit and run
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously small amount for car damage
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - the trivial damage amount undermines the moral weight of the hit-and-run.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: So what's the damage?

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: Two hundred dollars.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: Two hundred dollars? That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Mechanic
dialogue.line: Yeah, just a little scratch. I can buff it right out.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: A scratch? I hit this car going 40 miles an hour!

jokes.text: So, what are you doing this weekend?
jokes.setup: Jerry just paid for someone else's car damage
jokes.punchline: Immediately trying to ask out the victim
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry timing - making a romantic move right after handling the awkward car situation.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So, uh, that's all taken care of. The damage, the whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Listen, I was wondering... are you free Saturday night?

jokes.text: You are so good-looking.
jokes.setup: Becky rejecting Jerry for the hit-and-run
jokes.punchline: Jerry using Michael's 'better than God bless you' line
jokes.explanation: Another callback to the 'good-looking' line, now used as Jerry's desperate closing argument.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I have a line that's better than 'God bless you.' When someone sneezes, I say 'You're so good looking.'

jokes.text: You got a date with Becky Gelke?
jokes.setup: Kramer announces his date with Jerry's crush
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dismayed realization of the situation
jokes.explanation: Good ironic twist - Kramer gets the girl Jerry wanted through Jerry's own actions.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm going out with Elaine.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? You're going out with Elaine?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're going to dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I can't believe this. This is unbelievable.

jokes.text: A man is paralyzed, mentally, by a beautiful woman.
jokes.setup: Jerry's closing standup routine
jokes.punchline: Clinical description of male reaction to beauty
jokes.explanation: Solid observational standup connecting to the episode's theme of men making poor decisions around attractive women.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice how men are basically just a biological response to beauty? It's like we're not even thinking—we're just reacting. Our brains shut down completely. A woman walks by and suddenly we're cavemen. We lose all higher functioning. 'What was I saying? Who am I? What's my name?' It's involuntary. We can't help it. We're helpless.

jokes.text: All right, if she's next to the ratchet set and I had that ratchet set, I wonder if that would mean that... I better just buy that ratchet set.
jokes.setup: Describing male response to advertising with attractive women
jokes.punchline: The illogical thought process leading to purchase
jokes.explanation: Good breakdown of male advertising psychology with realistic internal monologue structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You see a beautiful woman in a car commercial, and you think, 'You know what? I should buy that car. Maybe she'll be in it.'

tmdb_overview: Jerry witnesses an accident--and is attracted to the victim and the perpetrator.
insight: Observational density peaks at 3.01 jokes per minute across 65 total gags.
